content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
159

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Know your target in the dependency graph (leandag)
18	
19	The blueprint dependency graph is queryable **read-only** — `archon` is on PATH, `--json` is parseable (the banner goes to stderr). Use it to ground your work instead of guessing:
20	
21	- `archon dag-query node --node <label> --json` — your target's blueprint entry, its `\lean{}` pin, and status. **If the `\lean{}` pin is a `…TODO.…` placeholder, the Lean declaration does not exist yet** — you must *create* it (the typed `sorry`, or the proof if your mode builds it), not "fill" a name that isn't in the environment.
22	- `archon dag-query ancestors --node <label> --json` — your target's full dependency closure. Build on deps that are actually done (`\leanok`); a dependency still marked ∞ / `sorry` is **not** a sound foundation — cite it only if it genuinely typechecks today.
23	- `archon dag-query gaps --json` — the ∞ holes (statements with no informal proof). If your target's cone hits one, the blueprint sketch is incomplete: flag it in your task_result rather than inventing a proof to paper over it.
24	- `archon dag-query unmatched --json` — Lean decls with no blueprint entry. After you add helpers, your new names surface here until they're blueprinted — that is the debt you must report (see Logging → "Needs blueprint entry").
25	
26	The label↔Lean mapping is the `\lean{}` annotation in the blueprint block; `leandag stats` / `leandag focus` also give a project-wide view. You never *write* the graph — that's the dag/plan/review agents' job — but reading it keeps you from proving against a hollow or unfinished foundation.
27	
28	## Protected declarations
29	
30	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
31	
32	## Talking to the user (TO_USER.md)
33	
34	`.archon/TO_USER.md` is a *persistent* shared notice board surfaced to the user as a banner. You may add a bullet **only** for something the user genuinely must act on that you hit while proving — e.g. a missing credential/dependency a sorry is gated on, or a `/- USER: -/` question you cannot resolve. Discipline:
35	
36	- **Concise + relevant**: keep the whole file ≤ 2–3 short bullets; before adding, read it and delete any bullet no longer true. Never a question queue — the loop never waits.
37	- **Concurrency**: provers run in parallel (one per file). To avoid clobbering a sibling lane's bullet, append your single bullet prefixed with your file (`- [Foo/Bar.lean] …`) rather than rewriting the whole file, and only when it's truly user-actionable. Routine "couldn't close this sorry" notes go in `task_results/<your_file>.md`, NOT here.
38	
39	## Avoid early termination
40	
41	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
42	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
43	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
44	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
45	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
46	
47	## Task completion criteria
48	
49	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
50	
51	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
52	2. Zero axioms introduced.
53	3. The file compiles cleanly.
54	
55	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
56	
57	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
58	
59	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
60	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
61	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
62	
63	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
64	
65	## When infrastructure is missing
66	
67	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
68	
69	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
70	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
71	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
72	
73	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
74	
75	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
76	
77	## Proof style
78	
79	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
80	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
81	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
82	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
83	- **List every new declaration you introduce under a `## Needs blueprint entry` heading in your task_result** (name, file, and the facts its proof relies on) — see Logging below. The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: the review agent and planner consume this list (and `archon dag-query unmatched`) to give each new declaration a blueprint block. An unreported helper is an invisible, isolated dependency that silently corrupts the frontier — flagging it is mandatory, not optional. (You never write the blueprint yourself; you only name what needs one.)
84	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
85	
86	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
87	
88	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
89	
90	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
91	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
92	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
93	
94	## LSP MCP tools
95	
96	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
97	
98	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
99	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
100	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
101	
102	## Search protocol
103	
104	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
105	
106	1. `lean_local_search` first.
107	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
108	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
109	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
110	
111	## Tooling traps
112	
113	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
114	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
115	
116	## Logging
117	
118	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
119	
120	```markdown
121	# Algebra/WLocal.lean

125	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
126	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
127	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing

129	### Attempt 2

131	- **Result:** RESOLVED
132	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap

134	## helper_bijective (line 78)
135	### Attempt 1
136	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
137	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
138	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
139	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
140	
141	## Needs blueprint entry
142	- `Algebra.WLocal.helper_bijective` (line 78) — new helper, no blueprint block yet. Uses: `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective`. Reviewer/planner: add a `\label` + `\lean` + `\uses` entry so the 1-to-1 correspondence holds.
143	```
144	
145	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter. **Always include the `## Needs blueprint entry` section when you added any new declaration** (omit it only if you added none) — it is how the loop keeps Lean ↔ blueprint at 1-to-1.
146	
147	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.

1	# Analogy: does Mathlib supply the functoriality of `p ↦ p_* p^* F`, or should the project keep hand-rolling `pushPullMap`/`pushPullMap_comp`?
2	
3	## Mode
4	api-alignment
5	
6	## Slug
7	pushpull
8	
9	## Iteration
10	001
11	
12	## Question
13	Does Mathlib already provide the functoriality of `p ↦ p_* p^* F` (or `p ↦ p_* p^*`)
14	for sheaves of modules on schemes, so we should NOT hand-roll `pushPullMap` /
15	`pushPullMap_comp`? (1) Is there a pseudofunctor→functor "straightening" giving an honest
16	`Functor (Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules` for a fixed `F`? (2) Is there a Mathlib idiom for the
17	cosimplicial object obtained by applying a (pseudo)functor to the simplicial nerve of a
18	cover arrow, letting the project build `CechNerve` from `coverCechNerve` WITHOUT first
19	assembling `G` and its `map_comp`? (3) If hand-rolled is right, is the `pushPullMap`
20	DEFINITION shape (two over-triangle `eqToHom` transports) the problem — would routing
21	through the adjunction transpose / `homEquiv` give a transport-light `map_comp` provable
22	by `pseudofunctor_associativity` without the defeq wall?
23	
24	## Project artifact(s)
25	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean:157-181` — `pushPullObj`, `pushPullMap`.
26	- `…:210-265` — `pushPullMap_id` (closed; template).
27	- `…:267-471` — the `pushPullMap_comp` recipe + dead-ends; bricks `pushPull_unit_mate`,
28	  `pushPull_unit_comp`, `pushforwardComp_hom_app_id`, `rawPushPullMap`, `pushPullMap_eq_raw`,
29	  `pushPull_transport_cancel`.
30	- `…:83-91` — `CechNerve` (the `sorry`); `…:124-135` — `coverArrow`, `coverCechNerve`.
31	
32	## Mathlib infrastructure that exists (verified)
33	
34	- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pseudofunctor : Pseudofunctor (LocallyDiscrete Scheme.{u}ᵒᵖ) (Adj Cat)`
35	  in `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Modules/Sheaf.lean`. Packages *both* the covariant
36	  (pushforward, right-adjoint) and contravariant (pullback, left-adjoint) functorialities,
37	  with full coherence: `pullbackComp`, `pushforwardComp`, `pullbackId`, `pushforwardId`,
38	  `pseudofunctor_associativity` (the pentagon), `pseudofunctor_left/right_unitality`.
39	- KEY: the pentagon/unitors are stated **only on the pullback (left-adjoint) side**
40	  (`pseudofunctor_associativity : (pullbackComp …).inv ≫ … = eqToHom _`). The
41	  pushforward-side coherences are *derived* via the mate calculus, NOT stated directly.
42	- `Scheme.Modules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv :
43	   conjugateEquiv ((adj g).comp (adj f)) (adj (f≫g)) (pullbackComp f g).inv = (pushforwardComp f g).hom`
44	   and `conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom` — the bridges turning pullback-side comparison isos
45	   into pushforward-side ones.
46	- `Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp_hom_app_app : ((pushforwardComp f g).hom.app M).app U = 𝟙 _` by `rfl`
47	   — the pushforward pseudofunctor is **strict** (its comparison 2-cells are identities on the nose).
48	- `CategoryTheory.conjugateEquiv` (`Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Adjunction/Mates.lean`): for
49	  `adj₁ : L₁⊣R₁`, `adj₂ : L₂⊣R₂`, the bijection `(L₂ ⟶ L₁) ≃ (R₁ ⟶ R₂)` (the mate/conjugate).
50	- **`CategoryTheory.conjugateEquiv_comp`** (same file): `conjugateEquiv adj₁ adj₂ α ≫
51	  conjugateEquiv adj₂ adj₃ β = conjugateEquiv adj₁ adj₃ (β ≫ α)`. This is *mate-of-composite =
52	  composite-of-mates* — the abstract functoriality identity the project is re-deriving by hand.
53	  Supporting: `conjugateEquiv_id`, `unit_conjugateEquiv`, `conjugateEquiv_apply_app`.
54	- `CategoryTheory.Pseudofunctor.Grothendieck` (`Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Bicategory/Grothendieck.lean`):
55	  the Grothendieck construction, but ONLY for `Pseudofunctor (LocallyDiscrete 𝒮) Cat`, and it
56	  builds the **total fibered category**, not the fixed-`F` functor on the base. Does NOT apply.
57	- `CategoryTheory.Over.lift` (`Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Comma/Over/Basic.lean`): lifts `D : J ⥤ T`
58	  with a cocone `D ⟶ const X` to `J ⥤ Over X` — the clean plumbing for the simplicial→over-X step.
59	
60	## Decisions identified
61	
62	### Decision: (Q1) Is there a pseudofunctor→functor straightening giving `G` for fixed `F`?
63	
64	- **Mathlib idiom**: NONE. The only straightening of a pseudofunctor in Mathlib is
65	  `Pseudofunctor.Grothendieck` / `CoGrothendieck` (`Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Bicategory/Grothendieck.lean`),
66	  which (a) requires the target bicategory to be `Cat` (the project's lands in `Adj Cat`), and
67	  (b) produces the *total category* `∫P` of a fibration, not the "evaluate at a fixed object,
68	  vary the base map" functor `(Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules`. There is no "fixed-object section" or
69	  "pointwise functor of a pseudofunctor" construction. Over-category pushforwards that *do*
70	  exist (`CategoryTheory.Over.pullback`, `ExponentiableMorphism.pushforward`,
71	  `LocallyCartesianClosed`) are the *topos/slice* pullback-pushforward, unrelated to the
72	  *module* pushforward `Scheme.Modules.pushforward`.
73	- **Project's path**: hand-roll `pushPullObj` + `pushPullMap` + functor laws.
74	- **Gap**: no idiom exists; project's path is the only option.
75	- **Verdict**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL. The hand-roll is genuine missing infrastructure,
76	  NOT a parallel API. Keep building it.
77	
78	### Decision: (Q2) Can `CechNerve` be built from `coverCechNerve` without assembling `G`?
79	
80	- **Mathlib idiom**: the simplicial→cosimplicial passage has clean plumbing —
81	  `coverCechNerve : SimplicialObject.Augmented Scheme` carries the augmentation
82	  `coverCechNerve.left ⟶ const X`, so `Over.lift` lifts it to `SimplexCategoryᵒᵖ ⥤ Over X`;
83	  then `(·).op ⋙ G : SimplexCategory ⥤ X.Modules` is exactly the (augmented) cosimplicial
84	  object. BUT this composition **requires `G` to be a genuine `Functor`** — i.e. it consumes
85	  `pushPullMap_comp`. Mathlib has no construction that applies a pseudofunctor to a Čech nerve
86	  and emits a cosimplicial object while dodging functoriality; functoriality is unavoidable
87	  somewhere.
88	- **Project's path**: build `G`, then compose (`relativeCechComplexOfNerve` already does the
89	  rest, coherence-free).
90	- **Gap**: identical to the only available route; the obstacle (`pushPullMap_comp`) is
91	  genuinely on the critical path. There is no Mathlib shortcut.
92	- **Verdict**: PROCEED. `pushPullMap_comp` IS the rate-limiter, as diagnosed — do not expect a
93	  bypass; invest in proving it (see Q3).
94	
95	### Decision: (Q3) Is the `pushPullMap` DEFINITION shape the problem? Mate route vs. pushforward-side pentagon grind.
96	
97	- **Mathlib idiom**: the **mate / conjugate calculus**. Mathlib structures pushforward
98	  (right-adjoint) functoriality as the *conjugate* of pullback (left-adjoint) functoriality —
99	  this is the whole design of `Scheme.Modules.pseudofunctor` (pentagon stated on pullback only;
100	  `conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv` bridges to pushforward). The composition law of any
101	  right-adjoint-valued contravariant functor is an instance of `conjugateEquiv_comp`
102	  (*mate-of-composite = composite-of-mates*), reduced — via injectivity of the `conjugateEquiv`
103	  bijection — to a *pullback-side* identity, which is exactly `pseudofunctor_associativity`.
104	- **Project's path**: the current proof fights the pentagon on the **pushforward side** with
105	  `erw`/`reassoc_of%`/`congr`, which `whnf`-explodes `pullbackComp` into its raw
106	  `TwoSquare.equivNatTrans`/`mateEquiv` mate definition (the documented dead-end). The two
107	  over-triangle `eqToHom` transports in `pushPullMap` are a *secondary* irritant (already
108	  dodged by `rawPushPullMap` + `subst`); the *primary* friction is staying on the pushforward
109	  side at all.
110	- **Gap**: divergent-with-cost. The project already discovered the correct entry points —
111	  `pushPull_unit_mate` (= `unit_conjugateEquiv` of the composite adjunction +
112	  `conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv`) and `pushPull_unit_comp` — but then *leaves* the mate
113	  algebra to grind a pushforward-side whiskered pentagon. The cost of not committing to the
114	  mate route: repeated `erw` `whnf` blow-ups, ~5 stalled iters.
115	- **Verdict**: ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB (on the proof/definition shape). Commit fully to the mate
116	  route.
117	
118	## Recommendation
119	
120	Keep the hand-rolled `G` (Q1: Mathlib has no straightening; Q2: functoriality is unavoidable
121	and on the critical path). But **refactor the proof of `pushPullMap_comp` to live entirely in
122	the mate/conjugate algebra**, never on the pushforward side:
123	
124	1. Recognize `pushPullMap F g` (for `g : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁`, `p₂ = a ≫ p₁`) as `(conjugateEquiv …
125	   χ_g).app F` whiskered/evaluated, where `χ_g` is the *pullback-side* comparison built from
126	   `(pullbackComp a p₁).inv` and the unit — i.e. the leg already isolated in
127	   `pushPull_unit_mate` / `conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv`. The pushforward strictness
128	   `pushforwardComp_hom_app_app = 𝟙` (by `rfl`) means every pushforward-side comparison is a
129	   definitional identity, so nothing nontrivial lives there.
130	2. Prove `pushPullMap_comp` by applying **`conjugateEquiv_comp`** (mate-of-composite), reducing
131	   it — via `Equiv`/`conjugateEquiv` injectivity — to the *pullback-side* identity
132	   `χ_{g≫h} = χ_h ≫ χ_g`. That identity is **`Scheme.Modules.pseudofunctor_associativity
133	   (f := h.left) (g := g.left) (h := Y₁.hom)`** used as a clean `rw`/`simp` (it is stated
134	   syntactically on `pullbackComp`/`pullbackId` *hom isos*, so it fires without `erw` and never
135	   unfolds the raw `mateEquiv` definition).
136	
137	The key alignment claim: the pentagon the project keeps losing on the pushforward side is
138	*already proven in Mathlib on the pullback side* (`pseudofunctor_associativity`), and the
139	mate-functoriality lemma `conjugateEquiv_comp` is the legal bridge. The defeq wall is an
140	artifact of doing the transport on the wrong (pushforward) side; the mate route stays in the
141	`conjugateEquiv` `≃`-algebra where composition is a clean algebraic identity. The bricks
142	`pushPull_unit_mate`, `pushPull_unit_comp`, `pushforwardComp_hom_app_id` the project already
143	built are exactly the right scaffolding — finish the route rather than reverting to a
144	pushforward-side `erw` grind.
145	
146	If a clean `conjugateEquiv`-mate *definition* of `pushPullMap` proves awkward to retrofit under
147	the frozen object `pushPullObj`, the minimal-change version is: keep `rawPushPullMap` +
148	`subst`, but after clearing the over-triangles, discharge the residual pentagon by rewriting
149	the goal into `conjugateEquiv` form (`pushPull_unit_comp` + `conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv`)
150	and closing with `conjugateEquiv_comp` + `pseudofunctor_associativity` + `Equiv.injective` —
151	deliberately avoiding `erw`/`congr` on any `pullbackComp` term.
152

1	\chapter{{\v C}ech computation of higher direct images $R^i f_*$ (unconditional)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
5	% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
6	% (Lemmas \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
7	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
8	% \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
9	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}) (read from
10	% references/stacks-coherent.tex).
11	
12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
15	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
16	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
17	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
18	derived functor, which in a formalisation requires the ambient category of
19	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives; that property is not currently
20	available for sheaves of modules over a sheaf of rings whose value category varies
21	over the site. The {\v C}ech approach developed here sidesteps the issue entirely:
22	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
23	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
24	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
25	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
26	
27	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
28	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
29	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
30	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
31	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
32	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
33	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
34	\[
35	  \begin{array}{ccc}
36	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
37	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
38	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
39	  \end{array}
40	\]
41	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
42	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
43	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
44	
45	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
46	
47	\begin{definition}
48	
49	\leanok
50	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
51	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
52	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
53	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
54	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
55	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
56	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
57	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
58	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
59	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
60	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
61	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
62	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
63	  % the unit ideal, see
64	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
65	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
66	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
67	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
68	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
69	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
70	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
71	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
72	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
73	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
74	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
75	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
76	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
77	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
78	  \[
79	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
80	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
81	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
82	  \]
83	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
84	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
85	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
86	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
87	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
88	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
89	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
90	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
91	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
92	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
93	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
94	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
95	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
96	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
97	\end{definition}
98	
99	\begin{definition}
100	
101	\leanok
102	[{\v C}ech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
103	  \label{def:cech_complex}
104	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
105	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
106	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
107	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
108	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
109	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
110	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
111	  % is identified with the complex
112	  % $$
113	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
114	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
115	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
116	  % \ldots
117	  % $$
118	  % We are asked to show that the extended complex
119	  % \begin{equation}
120	  % \label{equation-extended}
121	  % 0 \to
122	  % M \to
123	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
124	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
125	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
126	  % \ldots
127	  % \end{equation}
128	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
129	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact."
130	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
131	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
132	  vanishing).}
133	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
134	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
135	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
136	  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
137	  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
138	  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
139	  in degree \(p\) it is
140	  \[
141	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
142	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
143	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
144	  \]
145	  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the
146	  alternating sum \((d s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
147	  s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) of the
148	  restriction maps. Over an affine \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) with
149	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
150	  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex
151	  \(\prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to \cdots\) of
152	  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
153	  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
154	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite
155	  intersection instead of global sections; its terms are
156	  \(\prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*
157	  \bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr)\), and it is a complex in
158	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
159	  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated
160	  morphism is quasi-coherent.
161	\end{definition}
162	
163	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
164	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
165	
166	It is convenient to factor the construction of
167	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
168	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the entire mathematical
169	content that is not yet available off the shelf is isolated in the middle piece.
170	
171	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
172	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
173	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
174	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
175	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
176	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
177	\[
178	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
179	    \;=\;
180	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
181	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
182	\]
183	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
184	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
185	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
186	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
187	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
188	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
189	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
190	
191	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
192	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
193	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
194	\[
195	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
196	  \qquad
197	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
198	\]
199	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
200	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
201	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
202	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
203	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
204	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
205	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
206	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
207	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
208	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
209	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
210	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
211	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
212	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
213	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
214	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. This
215	functoriality is \emph{not} coupled to the project's tensor--pullback substrate;
216	it consumes only Mathlib's pseudofunctor packaging of pullback and pushforward.
217	Concretely, pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
218	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
219	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
220	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
221	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
222	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts about this
223	packaging: the mate identity expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback
224	comparison as the pushforward comparison (the lemma
225	\texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackComp\_inv}), its identity normalisation
226	\texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackId\_hom}, and the pseudofunctor unitor and pentagon
227	(associativity) coherences \texttt{pseudofunctor\_left\_unitality},
228	\texttt{pseudofunctor\_right\_unitality}, \texttt{pseudofunctor\_associativity}.
229	All of these are already available off the shelf, so the functoriality of the
230	relative-direct-image functor is a \emph{consumer of Mathlib's coherence},
231	independent of the project-local sheafification/pullback machinery of the
232	tensor--pullback substrate. The laws are nonetheless not formal trivialities: each
233	is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison isomorphisms along
234	the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and past the adjunction
235	unit, gluing them across two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions; the obstacle is the
236	bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
237	
238	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
239	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
240	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
241	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
242	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
243	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
244	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
245	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
246	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
247	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
248	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
249	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
250	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
251	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
252	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
253	that content is the pushforward/pullback pseudofunctor coherence supplied by
254	Mathlib (the comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
255	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
256	and their unitor/pentagon identities), not any project-local ingredient.
257	
258	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
259	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
260	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
261	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
262	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
263	
264	\begin{definition}\leanok
265	[Arrow of an affine cover]
266	  \label{def:cover_arrow}
267	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrow}
268	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an affine open cover. Its
269	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
270	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
271	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
272	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
273	  apply.
274	\end{definition}
275	
276	\begin{definition}\leanok
277	[Augmented {\v C}ech nerve of a cover]
278	  \label{def:cover_cech_nerve}
279	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerve}
280	  \uses{def:cover_arrow}
281	  The augmented {\v C}ech nerve of the cover arrow of
282	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_arrow}: the augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
283	  \(X\) whose degree-\(p\) object is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
284	  \[
285	    \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
286	      = \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
287	        U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
288	  \]
289	  with augmentation the cover map to \(X\). It exists unconditionally because the
290	  category of schemes has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the
291	  {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow). This is the geometric backbone (1).
292	\end{definition}
293	
294	\begin{definition}\leanok
295	[Push--pull object map]
296	  \label{def:push_pull_obj}
297	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObj}
298	  The object map of the push--pull functor \(G\): to an \(X\)-scheme
299	  \(p : Y \to X\) it assigns
300	  \[
301	    G(Y, p) \;=\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
302	  \]
303	  the pushforward along \(p\) of the pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\).
304	\end{definition}
305	
306	\begin{definition}\leanok
307	[Push--pull comparison map]
308	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap}
310	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
311	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
312	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
313	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
314	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
315	  \[
316	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
317	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
318	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
319	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
320	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
321	      \;=\;
322	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
323	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
324	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
325	  \]
326	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
327	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
328	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
329	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
330	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
331	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
332	\end{definition}
333	
334	\begin{lemma}
335	[Push--pull functor — identity law]
336	  \label{lem:push_pull_id}
337	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id}
338	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}
339	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} respects identities:
340	  \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)}\) for every \(X\)-scheme \(Y\).
341	\end{lemma}
342	
343	\begin{proof}
344	  A statement purely about the pushforward/pullback comparison isomorphisms, with no
345	  sectionwise content. Rewrite the head \(p_*(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward
346	  comparison as the conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison
347	  (\texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackComp\_inv}); the identity leg then collapses by the
348	  identity normalisation \texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackId\_hom} together with the right
349	  unitality \texttt{pseudofunctor\_right\_unitality}, and the two over-triangle
350	  \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions cancel against the unitality coercion.
351	\end{proof}
352	
353	\begin{lemma}
354	[Push--pull functor — composition law]
355	  \label{lem:push_pull_comp}
356	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp}
357	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
358	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} is contravariantly functorial:
359	  for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of \(X\)-schemes,
360	  \[
361	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g).
362	  \]
363	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} this assembles
364	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a functor
365	  \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); composing \(G\) with
366	  the backbone of Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
367	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.
368	\end{lemma}
369	
370	\begin{proof}
371	  \uses{lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
372	  The composition law of any right-adjoint-valued contravariant assignment is an instance
373	  of \emph{mate-of-composite \(=\) composite-of-mates}. Concretely, the pushforward
374	  (right-adjoint) comparison is the conjugate of the pullback (left-adjoint) comparison, and
375	  the general identity
376	  \[
377	    \mathrm{conjugateEquiv}(\mathrm{adj}_1,\mathrm{adj}_2)\,\alpha \circ
378	    \mathrm{conjugateEquiv}(\mathrm{adj}_2,\mathrm{adj}_3)\,\beta
379	    = \mathrm{conjugateEquiv}(\mathrm{adj}_1,\mathrm{adj}_3)\,(\beta \circ \alpha)
380	  \]
381	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.conjugateEquiv\_comp}, \texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Adjunction/Mates.lean})
382	  is exactly the content of the composition law. Using injectivity of the
383	  \(\mathrm{conjugateEquiv}\) bijection, the claim reduces to the corresponding identity on
384	  the \emph{pullback} side, which is the pseudofunctor pentagon
385	  \texttt{pseudofunctor\_associativity}; the composite adjunction unit is handled by the mate core
386	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_unit_mate} (and its solved form
387	  \texttt{pushPull\_unit\_comp}), and the over-triangle \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) transports are
388	  discharged by Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}.
389	\end{proof}
390	
391	\begin{lemma}
392	[Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push--pull head]
393	  \label{lem:push_pull_unit_mate}
394	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_mate}
395	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
396	  For composable scheme morphisms \(f : A \to B\), \(p : B \to Z\) and a
397	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module \(N\), the adjunction unit \(\eta^p_N\) of
398	  \(p^* \dashv p_*\) at \(N\), followed by \(p_*\) of the unit \(\eta^f\) for
399	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) at \(p^*N\), followed by the pushforward comparison
400	  \(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\), equals the unit \(\eta^{f \circ p}_N\) for the
401	  composite followed by \((f \circ p)_*\) applied to the inverse pullback
402	  comparison \((f \circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\):
403	  \[
404	    \eta^p_N \;\circ\; p_*(\eta^f_{p^*N}) \;\circ\;
405	      \bigl(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\bigr)
406	    \;=\;
407	    \eta^{f \circ p}_N \;\circ\; (f \circ p)_*\bigl((f\circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\bigr)^{-1}.
408	  \]
409	  This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
410	  \(\eta^{f \circ p}\) into the iterated units \(\eta^p, \eta^f\); it is the
411	  reusable ingredient consumed by the functoriality (pentagon) law of the
412	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}.
413	\end{lemma}
414	
415	\begin{proof}
416	  Proved directly in Lean.
417	\end{proof}
418	
419	\begin{lemma}
420	[Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push--pull tail]
421	  \label{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
422	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_transport_cancel}
423	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
424	  Let \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\), \(p_1 : Y_1 \to X\), \(p_2 : Y_2 \to X\) satisfy the
425	  over-triangle \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\), and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
426	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the pullback-comparison leg of the push--pull map,
427	  conjugated by the two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions along the over-triangle,
428	  collapses to the transport-light form
429	  \[
430	    e_1 \;\circ\; p_{2*}\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
431	      \;\circ\; e_2
432	    \;=\;
433	    (\bar g p_1)_*\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
434	      \;\circ\; e_3,
435	  \]
436	  where \(e_1, e_2, e_3\) are the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions induced by
437	  \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\). It states the over-triangle cancellation in the
438	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} with the
439	  over-triangle equality as a free hypothesis, so a single substitution turns the
440	  transports into identities; it is the reusable pre-coherence brick for the
441	  composition (pentagon) law.
442	\end{lemma}
443	
444	\begin{proof}
445	  Proved directly in Lean.
446	\end{proof}

448	\begin{definition}\leanok
449	[Relative {\v C}ech complex from a nerve]
450	  \label{def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
451	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.relativeCechComplexOfNerve}
452	  \uses{def:cech_nerve}
453	  Given \(f : X \to S\) and an augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
454	  \(N\), the \emph{relative {\v C}ech cochain complex} in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\)
455	  obtained by forgetting the augmentation of \(N\), pushing the resulting
456	  cosimplicial object forward degreewise along \(f\), and taking the
457	  alternating-coface-map cochain complex. This passage uses only the preadditivity
458	  of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) and \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence; it is
459	  the coherence-free plumbing (3) that turns the nerve of
460	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} into the {\v C}ech complex.
461	\end{definition}

464	
465	\begin{lemma}
466	
467	\leanok
468	[Standard-cover {\v C}ech-complex vanishing on affines]
469	  \label{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
470	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechAcyclic.affine}
471	  \uses{def:cech_complex}
472	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
473	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover
474	  % {\v C}ech vanishing) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L44--52).
475	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
476	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
477	  % Let $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$ be a standard
478	  % open covering of an affine open of $X$.
479	  % Then $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for
480	  % all $p > 0$."
481	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
482	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
483	  vanishing).}
484	  Let \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) be an affine scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a
485	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module, and
486	  \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) a standard open cover (so the
487	  \(f_i \in A\) generate the unit ideal). Then the {\v C}ech complex
488	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
489	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} has vanishing cohomology in all positive
490	  degrees:
491	  \[
492	    \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p > 0.
493	  \]
494	  Equivalently, the extended complex
495	  \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}}
496	  \to \cdots\) of localisations is exact. This is the standard-cover {\v C}ech
497	  vanishing only; the sheaf-cohomology Serre vanishing
498	  \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) is the separate
499	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} below, obtained from this complex
500	  vanishing by the basis-comparison Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}.
501	\end{lemma}
502	
503	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Write $U = \Spec(A)$ for some ring $A$.
504	% In other words, $f_1, \ldots, f_n$ are elements of $A$
505	% which generate the unit ideal of $A$.
506	% Write $\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}$ for some $A$-module $M$.
507	% ...
508	% It suffices to show that (\ref{equation-extended})
509	% is exact after localizing at a prime $\mathfrak p$, see
510	% Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-characterize-zero-local}.
511	% In fact we will show that the extended complex localized
512	% at $\mathfrak p$ is homotopic to zero.
513	% There exists an index $i$ such that $f_i \not \in \mathfrak p$.
514	% Choose and fix such an element $i_{\text{fix}}$. ...
515	% Let us define a homotopy
516	% $$
517	% h :
518	% \prod\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_{p + 1}}
519	% M_{f_{i_0} \ldots f_{i_{p + 1}}, \mathfrak p}
520	% \longrightarrow
521	% \prod\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_p}
522	% M_{f_{i_0} \ldots f_{i_p}, \mathfrak p}
523	% $$
524	% by the rule
525	% $$
526	% h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\text{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}
527	% $$
528	% ... We compute ... $(dh + hd)(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ ...
529	% This proves the identity map is homotopic to zero as desired."
530	\begin{proof}
531	  \uses{def:cech_complex}
532	  Write \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) for an
533	  \(A\)-module \(M\). By Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} the {\v C}ech complex of
534	  the standard cover is the complex of localisations
535	  \[
536	    \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \;\to\;
537	    \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \;\to\;
538	    \prod_{i_0 i_1 i_2} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1} f_{i_2}} \;\to\; \cdots,
539	  \]
540	  and the claim \(\check{H}^p = 0\) for \(p > 0\) is equivalent to exactness of the
541	  extended complex \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \cdots\) (whose
542	  truncation is studied in Algebra, Lemma \texttt{algebra-lemma-cover-module}).
543	  Exactness may be checked after localising at an arbitrary prime
544	  \(\mathfrak{p} \subset A\) (Algebra, Lemma
545	  \texttt{algebra-lemma-characterize-zero-local}). Choose an index
546	  \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) with \(f_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}} \notin \mathfrak{p}\); then
547	  \(M_{f_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}}, \mathfrak{p}} = M_{\mathfrak{p}}\), and the
548	  prescription \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\mathrm{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}\)
549	  defines a contracting homotopy of the localised extended complex: a direct
550	  computation gives \((dh + hd)(s) = s\), so the identity is homotopic to zero and
551	  the localised complex is exact. As exactness holds after every localisation, the
552	  extended complex is exact and \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for
553	  \(p > 0\).
554	\end{proof}

556	\begin{lemma}
557	[Serre vanishing on affines]
558	  \label{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}
559	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.affine_serre_vanishing}

564	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Serre vanishing: Higher cohomology vanishes on affine schemes
565	  % for quasi-coherent modules.

567	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.

569	  % $H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$."
570	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KG
571	  (\texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}, Serre vanishing on
572	  affines).}
573	  Let \(U\) be an affine scheme and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
574	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module. Then the sheaf cohomology of \(\mathcal{F}\) vanishes in
575	  all positive degrees:
576	  \[
577	    H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p > 0.
578	  \]
579	\end{lemma}

584	% the affine opens of $X$.
585	% As our set $\text{Cov}$ of open coverings we are going to use the standard
586	% open coverings of affine opens of $X$.
587	% Next we check that conditions (1), (2) and (3) of
588	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis}
589	% hold. Note that the intersection of standard opens in an affine is
590	% another standard open. Hence property (1) holds.

592	% of $\mathcal{B}$, see
593	% Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-standard-open}.

599	  Take the affine opens of \(U\) as a basis \(\mathcal{B}\) of the topology and the
600	  standard open covers of affine opens as the admissible covers. The intersection of
601	  two standard opens in an affine is again a standard open, so \(\mathcal{B}\) is
602	  closed under the relevant intersections; and the standard covers form a cofinal
603	  system among the open covers of any basic open. The standard-cover {\v C}ech
604	  vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} supplies the acyclicity
605	  hypothesis. The {\v C}ech-to-cohomology comparison on a basis
606	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}) then identifies the sheaf cohomology
607	  of \(\mathcal{F}\) on each basic open with its {\v C}ech cohomology for a standard
608	  cover, whence \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\).
609	\end{proof}

642	  % As our basis $\mathcal{B}$ for the topology of $X$ we are going to use
643	  % the affine opens of $X$.

645	  % open coverings of affine opens of $X$.
646	  % Next we check that conditions (1), (2) and (3) of
647	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis}
648	  % hold. Note that the intersection of standard opens in an affine is

657	  \texttt{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis} (as applied in the proof of
658	  \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}).}
659	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\mathcal{B}\) be a basis for its topology, and let a
660	  set of admissible open covers be given, subject to: (1) \(\mathcal{B}\) is closed
661	  under finite intersection; (2) for each \(B \in \mathcal{B}\) the admissible covers
662	  of \(B\) are cofinal among all open covers of \(B\); and (3) for every admissible
663	  cover \(\mathcal{U}\) of every \(B \in \mathcal{B}\), every quasi-coherent
664	  \(\mathcal{F}\), and every \(p > 0\), the {\v C}ech cohomology vanishes,
665	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\). Then for every \(B \in \mathcal{B}\)

677	  sequence for a cover \(\mathcal{U}\) of \(B \in \mathcal{B}\) has its higher rows
678	  killed by hypothesis (3) applied to the elements of \(\mathcal{B}\) appearing as
679	  the finite intersections of \(\mathcal{U}\) (these lie in \(\mathcal{B}\) by (1)),
680	  so it collapses and the edge map \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \to
681	  H^p(B, \mathcal{F})\) is an isomorphism; cofinality (2) makes the identification
682	  independent of the chosen admissible cover. This is the standard Čech-comparison
683	  criterion on a basis, applied with \(\mathcal{B}\) the affine opens and the
684	  admissible covers the standard covers, condition (3) being the standard-cover
685	  vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}. \emph{The general
686	  basis-comparison criterion for sheaves of modules on a scheme is not yet available
687	  in Mathlib and is recorded here as a to-build dependency.}
688	\end{proof}

690	\section{The {\v C}ech complex computes $R^i f_*$}
691	
692	The comparison is proved by the \emph{acyclic-resolution} (Cartan--Leray) route. The
693	augmented {\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) whose terms are
694	acyclic for \(f_*\); the abstract homological-algebra
695	Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} then identifies the derived
696	functor with the cohomology of the complex obtained by applying \(f_*\). The two
697	geometric inputs are isolated in the following sub-lemmas.
698	
699	\begin{lemma}
700	[The augmented {\v C}ech complex is a resolution]
701	  \label{lem:cech_augmented_resolution}
702	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechAugmented_exact}
703	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
704	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
705	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof: exactness of the
706	  % extended {\v C}ech complex) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L68--82).
707	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We are asked to show that the extended complex
708	  % \begin{equation}
709	  % \label{equation-extended}
710	  % 0 \to
711	  % M \to
712	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
713	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
714	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
715	  % \ldots
716	  % \end{equation}
717	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
718	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact.
719	  % It suffices to show that (\ref{equation-extended})
720	  % is exact after localizing at a prime $\mathfrak p$, see
721	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-characterize-zero-local}."
722	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
723	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof).}
724	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
725	  \(\mathfrak{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with all
726	  intersections affine. Form the augmented {\v C}ech complex on \(X\),
727	  \[
728	    0 \;\to\; \mathcal{F} \;\to\; \mathcal{C}^0 \;\to\; \mathcal{C}^1 \;\to\;
729	    \mathcal{C}^2 \;\to\; \cdots,
730	    \qquad
731	    \mathcal{C}^p \;=\; \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
732	      (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*\bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
733	  \]
734	  the degree-\(p\) term being the degree-\(p\) object of the {\v C}ech nerve of
735	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}, with augmentation the canonical map
736	  \(\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{C}^0\). This augmented complex is exact; that is, the
737	  {\v C}ech nerve is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\).
738	\end{lemma}
739	
740	\begin{proof}
741	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
742	  Exactness of a complex of sheaves is a local question on \(X\), so it may be
743	  checked on the stalks, equivalently over each affine open of a basis. Restricting
744	  to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) on which \(\mathcal{F}|_U =
745	  \widetilde{M}\) and over which the cover restricts to a standard cover \(U =
746	  \bigcup D(f_i)\), the augmented {\v C}ech complex becomes the extended complex of
747	  localisations
748	  \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to
749	  \cdots\). Its exactness is exactly the standard-cover vanishing of
750	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}, witnessed by the prime-local contracting
751	  homotopy \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\mathrm{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}\). As this
752	  holds over every affine of the basis, the augmented complex is exact on \(X\).
753	\end{proof}
754	
755	\begin{lemma}
756	[Presheaf description of the higher direct images]
757	  \label{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}
758	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage_isSheafify_presheafCohomology}
759	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}

764	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to Y$ be a morphism of ringed spaces.
765	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.

774	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Tag 01XJ
775	  (\texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}).}
776	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{G}\) an
777	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For every \(k \geq 0\) the higher direct image
778	  \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G} = (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\,(\mathcal{G})\) is the sheaf
779	  on \(S\) associated to the presheaf
780	  \[
781	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^k\bigl(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
782	    \qquad V \subseteq S \text{ open},
783	  \]
784	  with the evident restriction maps. In particular \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G}\) vanishes
785	  iff \(H^k(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0\) for every \(V\) in a basis of
786	  the topology of \(S\); since the affine opens form such a basis, it suffices to
787	  check the vanishing on affine \(V\). This is the local-on-the-base description
788	  through which \(f_*\)-acyclicity is reduced to absolute cohomology vanishing on
