9	/-!
10	# Čech computation of the higher direct images `Rⁱ f_*` (unconditional)
11	
12	This file constructs the higher derived direct images `Rⁱ f_* F` for `i ≥ 1`
13	**without appealing to injective resolutions** in the category of sheaves of
14	modules. The companion `Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean` defines `Rⁱ f_*` as a
15	right derived functor, which requires the ambient category of `O_X`-modules to
16	have enough injectives — a property not currently available for sheaves of
17	modules over a sheaf of rings whose value category varies over the site. The
18	Čech approach developed here sidesteps the issue: it computes `Rⁱ f_* F` as the
19	cohomology of an explicit complex built from the pushforwards of `F` over the
20	finite intersections of an affine open cover, producing an **unconditional**
21	construction of `Rⁱ f_*` for quasi-coherent `F` and separated quasi-compact `f`.
22	
23	Throughout, `f : X ⟶ S` is a quasi-compact, separated morphism of schemes (so
24	all finite intersections of an affine open cover of `X` are again affine), and
25	`F : X.Modules` is a quasi-coherent `O_X`-module. A base change of `f` along
26	`g : S' ⟶ S` is recorded by a cartesian square
27	```
28	  X' --g'--> X
29	  |f'        |f
30	  v          v
31	  S' --g---> S
32	```
33	with `F' = (g')^* F` the pullback of `F` to `X'`.
34	
35	The six main declarations are:
36	
37	* `AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve` — the (augmented) Čech nerve of an affine open
38	  cover, an augmented cosimplicial object of `O_X`-modules.
39	* `AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex` — the relative Čech complex in `QCoh(S)`, a
40	  cochain complex of `O_S`-modules whose degree-`p` term is the product of the
41	  pushforwards of `F` over the `(p+1)`-fold intersections of the cover.
42	* `AlgebraicGeometry.CechAcyclic.affine` — Čech acyclicity on affines: the Čech
43	  complex of a standard cover of an affine scheme has vanishing cohomology in
44	  all positive degrees (Serre vanishing for quasi-coherent sheaves on affines).
45	* `AlgebraicGeometry.cech_computes_higherDirectImage` — the cohomology of the
46	  relative Čech complex is canonically isomorphic to `Rⁱ f_* F` wherever the
47	  derived functor is defined.
48	
49	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage.tex`.
50	
51	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, §Čech cohomology of quasi-coherent
52	sheaves and §Quasi-coherence of higher direct images; Tags 02KE
53	(`lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent`), 02KG
54	(`lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero`).
55	-/

67	/-- **Čech nerve of an affine open cover** (Stacks, Cohomology of Schemes, §Čech
68	cohomology of quasi-coherent sheaves).
69	
70	For a scheme `X`, a finite affine open cover `𝒰 : X = ⋃ Uᵢ` and a quasi-coherent
71	sheaf `F`, the *Čech nerve* is the augmented cosimplicial object of
72	`O_X`-modules whose object in simplicial degree `p` is the product, over the
73	`(p+1)`-tuples `(i₀,…,i_p)` of indices, of the direct images
74	`(j_{i₀…i_p})_* (F|_{U_{i₀…i_p}})` of the restriction of `F` to the
75	`(p+1)`-fold intersection `U_{i₀…i_p} = U_{i₀} ∩ ⋯ ∩ U_{i_p}` along the open
76	immersion `j_{i₀…i_p} : U_{i₀…i_p} ↪ X`. Faces are the restriction maps that
77	omit one index, degeneracies repeat one index, and the augmentation in degree
78	`-1` is `F` itself on all of `X`. When `X` is separated each intersection
79	`U_{i₀…i_p}` is affine.
80	
81	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
82	`lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial`. -/
83	noncomputable def CechNerve (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) (F : X.Modules) :
84	    CosimplicialObject.Augmented X.Modules :=
85	  -- Construction (Stacks): the augmented cosimplicial object
86	  -- `[p] ↦ ∏_{(i₀,…,i_p)} (j_{i₀…i_p})_* (F|_{U_{i₀…i_p}})` with faces the
87	  -- index-omitting restriction maps, degeneracies the index-repeating maps, and
88	  -- augmentation `F`. Requires the nerve of the cover (intersection products of
89	  -- pushforwards) as a functor out of `SimplexCategory`, currently absent from
90	  -- Mathlib for `Scheme.Modules`.
91	  sorry

93	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — scheme-level Čech nerve backbone
94	
95	The genuine construction of the {\v C}ech nerve `CechNerve` factors through two
96	ingredients that are independent of one another:
97	
98	* a *geometric* backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of the cover, an
99	  augmented simplicial **scheme** over `X` (the iterated fibre powers of
100	  `∐ᵢ Uᵢ` over `X`), which exists unconditionally because `Scheme` has all

100	  `∐ᵢ Uᵢ` over `X`), which exists unconditionally because `Scheme` has all
101	  finite limits; and
102	* a *push-pull* functor `(Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules`, `(Y, p) ↦ p_* p^* F`, that
103	  turns the simplicial scheme over `X` into the cosimplicial `O_X`-module
104	  `CechNerve`.
105	
106	The backbone (`coverArrow`, `coverCechNerve`) is built here axiom-clean. The
107	push-pull functor is the remaining gap: its `map_comp` requires the
108	`pushforwardComp` / `pullbackComp` coherence isomorphisms (the same coherence
109	quagmire active in `Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean`), so `CechNerve` itself is
110	left as the single genuine hole.
111	
112	Independently of the nerve, the passage *from* an augmented cosimplicial
113	`O_X`-module *to* the relative {\v C}ech cochain complex in `QCoh(S)` is pure,
114	coherence-free plumbing (`relativeCechComplexOfNerve`): forget the augmentation,
115	push forward along `f` via `CosimplicialObject.whiskering`, and take the
116	alternating coface-map cochain complex. We record it here so that `CechComplex`
117	is *defined* in terms of `CechNerve` — closing `CechNerve` axiom-clean
118	immediately yields an axiom-clean `CechComplex`. -/
119	
120	/-- The arrow `∐ᵢ Uᵢ ⟶ X` (`Sigma.desc 𝒰.f`) attached to an open cover `𝒰` of a
121	scheme `X`. Its augmented {\v C}ech nerve is the geometric backbone of the
122	relative {\v C}ech complex. Project-local: packages the cover as a single arrow
123	so the existing `Arrow.augmentedCechNerve` machinery applies. -/
124	noncomputable def coverArrow (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) : Arrow Scheme.{u} :=
125	  Arrow.mk (Sigma.desc 𝒰.f)
126	
127	/-- The augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an open cover `𝒰`, as an augmented
128	simplicial scheme over `X`: in simplicial degree `p` it is the `(p+1)`-fold
129	fibre power of `∐ᵢ Uᵢ` over `X`, i.e. `∐_{(i₀,…,i_p)} U_{i₀} ×ₓ ⋯ ×ₓ U_{i_p}`,
130	with augmentation the cover map to `X`. Exists unconditionally because `Scheme`
131	has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks used by
132	`Arrow.augmentedCechNerve`). Project-local geometric backbone for `CechNerve`. -/
133	noncomputable def coverCechNerve (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) :
134	    SimplicialObject.Augmented Scheme.{u} :=
135	  (coverArrow 𝒰).augmentedCechNerve
136	
137	/-! ### Push–pull functor `G : (Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules` — object and morphism bricks
138	
139	The geometric backbone above is lifted to a cosimplicial `O_X`-module by the
140	*relative-direct-image functor on the over-category*
141	```
142	  G : (Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules,   (Y, p) ↦ p_* p^* F,
143	```
144	sending an `X`-scheme `p : Y ⟶ X` to the pushforward along `p` of the pullback
145	`p^* F`. We record here the two *pre-coherence* bricks of `G` — its action on
146	objects (`pushPullObj`, the planner's `Gobj`) and on morphisms (`pushPullMap`,
147	the planner's `Gmap`) — both axiom-clean and free of any functor law. The functor
148	laws `G(𝟙) = 𝟙` and `G(g ≫ h) = G(h) ≫ G(g)` are a *consumer* of the
149	pushforward/pullback composition coherence (`pushforwardComp` / `pullbackComp` and
150	their unitor/pentagon identities) and are deferred; see the note after
151	`pushPullMap`. -/
152	
153	/-- The object map of the push–pull functor `G : (Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules`,
154	`(Y, p) ↦ p_* p^* F`. Sends an `X`-scheme `Y` (with structure map `Y.hom : Y.left
155	⟶ X`) to the pushforward along `Y.hom` of the pullback of `F`. Project-local
156	object brick of the {\v C}ech push–pull functor (the planner's `Gobj`). -/
157	noncomputable def pushPullObj (F : X.Modules) (Y : Over X) : X.Modules :=
158	  (pushforward Y.hom).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)
159	
160	/-- The morphism map of the push–pull functor `G : (Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules`. For a
161	morphism `g : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁` of `X`-schemes (so `g.left ≫ Y₁.hom = Y₂.hom`, the
162	over-triangle `Over.w g`), the contravariant functor produces the restriction
163	comparison `pushPullObj F Y₁ ⟶ pushPullObj F Y₂`. It is the three-step composite:
164	the unit `η` of the adjunction `pullback g.left ⊣ pushforward g.left`, the
165	pushforward comparison `(pushforwardComp g.left Y₁.hom).hom`, and the pushforward
166	of the pullback comparison `(pullbackComp g.left Y₁.hom).hom`, glued by two
167	`eqToHom` transports along the over-triangle `Over.w g`. No functor law is used:
168	this is a reusable pre-coherence brick (the planner's `Gmap`). -/
169	noncomputable def pushPullMap (F : X.Modules) {Y₁ Y₂ : Over X} (g : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁) :
170	    pushPullObj F Y₁ ⟶ pushPullObj F Y₂ :=
171	  (pushforward Y₁.hom).map
172	      ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g.left).unit.app
173	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y₁.hom).obj F)) ≫
174	    (pushforwardComp g.left Y₁.hom).hom.app
175	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g.left).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y₁.hom).obj F)) ≫
176	    eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (pushforward q).obj
177	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g.left).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y₁.hom).obj F)))
178	      (Over.w g)) ≫
179	    (pushforward Y₂.hom).map ((pullbackComp g.left Y₁.hom).hom.app F) ≫
180	    eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (pushforward Y₂.hom).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F))
181	      (Over.w g))
182	
183	/- **The functor laws `pushPullMap_id` / `pushPullMap_comp`.**
184	Assembling `pushPullObj` / `pushPullMap` into the functor `G : (Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤
185	X.Modules` requires
186	```
187	  pushPullMap_id   : pushPullMap F (𝟙 Y) = 𝟙 (pushPullObj F Y)          -- DONE (below)
188	  pushPullMap_comp : pushPullMap F (g ≫ h) = pushPullMap F h ≫ pushPullMap F g  -- remaining
189	```
190	Both are pure pushforward/pullback *coherence* statements with no sectionwise
191	content of their own. The route is the adjunction-mate calculus: the
192	`unit ≫ pushforwardComp.hom` head of `pushPullMap` is governed by `unit_conjugateEquiv`
193	and `conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv` / `conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom`, and the `eqToHom`
194	transports are discharged against the pseudofunctor unitor/pentagon identities
195	(`pseudofunctor_right_unitality` for `id`, `pseudofunctor_associativity` for `comp`, all in
196	`Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Modules/Sheaf.lean`).
197	
198	`pushPullMap_id` is closed axiom-clean below. A plain `simp` (even with the coherence lemmas)
199	makes no progress on the head `unit ≫ pushforwardComp.hom`, and the sectionwise `ext` route
200	exposes the pullback adjunction unit (not sectionwise trivial); the working proof instead routes
201	through `unit_conjugateEquiv` of the identity adjunction (`star`), the right-unitality of the
202	pullback comparison (`hru`), and a `Scheme.Modules.hom_ext; intro U; rfl` collapse of the
203	sectionwise-trivial pushforward coercion (`hpf`). `pushPullMap_comp` follows the same template
204	with `pseudofunctor_associativity` (the pentagon) in place of right-unitality — see the comment
205	above its (currently unfilled) statement. -/
206	
207	/-! ### Functor laws of the push–pull functor `G` -/
208	
209	/-- Identity law of the push–pull functor `G`. -/
210	lemma pushPullMap_id (F : X.Modules) (Y : Over X) :
211	    pushPullMap F (𝟙 Y) = 𝟙 (pushPullObj F Y) := by
212	  -- `star`: the unit-triangle for the identity adjunction `pullback 𝟙 ⊣ pushforward 𝟙`.
213	  have star := unit_conjugateEquiv (Adjunction.id (C := Y.left.Modules))
214	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (𝟙 Y.left))
215	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackId Y.left).hom ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)
216	  rw [Scheme.Modules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom] at star
217	  simp only [Adjunction.id_unit, NatTrans.id_app, Functor.id_obj] at star
218	  -- `hru`: right-unitality of the pullback pseudofunctor, applied at `F`.
219	  have hru := Scheme.Modules.pseudofunctor_right_unitality (X := Y.left) (f := Y.hom)
220	  have hru2 := congrArg (fun t => NatTrans.app t F) hru
221	  simp only [NatTrans.comp_app, Functor.whiskerLeft_app, Functor.rightUnitor_hom_app] at hru2
222	  have hpf : (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp (𝟙 Y.left) Y.hom).hom.app
223	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (𝟙 Y.left)).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)) ≫
224	      eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward q).obj
225	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (𝟙 Y.left)).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)))
226	        (Category.id_comp Y.hom)) =
227	      (Scheme.Modules.pushforward Y.hom).map ((Scheme.Modules.pushforwardId Y.left).hom.app
228	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (𝟙 Y.left)).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F))) := by
229	    apply Scheme.Modules.hom_ext
230	    intro U; rfl
231	  -- the unit zig-zag for the identity adjunction collapses on `M`
232	  have hzig : (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (𝟙 Y.left)).unit.app
233	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F) ≫
234	      (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardId Y.left).hom.app
235	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (𝟙 Y.left)).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)) ≫
236	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackId Y.left).hom.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F) =
237	      𝟙 ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F) := by
238	    have hnat := (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardId Y.left).hom.naturality
239	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackId Y.left).hom.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F))
240	    simp only [Functor.id_map] at hnat
241	    erw [← hnat, ← reassoc_of% star]
242	    exact Iso.inv_hom_id_app _ _
243	  -- the pullback comparison + the over-triangle transport collapse via right-unitality
244	  have hib_inner : (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (𝟙 Y.left) Y.hom).hom.app F ≫
245	      eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F) (Category.id_comp Y.hom)) =
246	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackId Y.left).hom.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F) := by
247	    rw [eqToHom_app] at hru2
248	    rw [← hru2, ← Category.assoc, Iso.hom_inv_id_app]; simp
249	  have hib : (Scheme.Modules.pushforward Y.hom).map
250	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (𝟙 Y.left) Y.hom).hom.app F) ≫
251	      eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward Y.hom).obj
252	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) (Category.id_comp Y.hom)) =
253	      (Scheme.Modules.pushforward Y.hom).map
254	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackId Y.left).hom.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)) := by
255	    have he : eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward Y.hom).obj
256	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) (Category.id_comp Y.hom)) =
257	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward Y.hom).map
258	          (eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)
259	            (Category.id_comp Y.hom))) := by
260	      rw [eqToHom_map]
261	    rw [he, ← Functor.map_comp]; exact congrArg _ hib_inner
262	  -- assemble
263	  simp only [pushPullMap, Over.id_left]
264	  erw [reassoc_of% hpf, hib, ← Functor.map_comp]
265	  erw [hzig, CategoryTheory.Functor.map_id]; rfl
266	
267	/- **Composition law of the push–pull functor `G` (contravariant) — remaining.**
268	The identity law `pushPullMap_id` above is closed axiom-clean by the mate calculus
269	(`unit_conjugateEquiv` + `conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom` + `pseudofunctor_right_unitality`).
270	The composition law
271	```
272	  pushPullMap_comp : pushPullMap F (g ≫ h) = pushPullMap F h ≫ pushPullMap F g
273	```
274	follows the *same* template one step up — but with the pentagon
275	`Scheme.Modules.pseudofunctor_associativity (f := g.left) (g := h.left) (h := Y₁.hom)`
276	(verified to typecheck) in place of `pseudofunctor_right_unitality`, the composite-adjunction
277	unit expansion `Adjunction.comp_unit_app` for `η_{g.left ≫ h.left}`, and
278	`Adjunction.unit_naturality` to slide the inner unit past the outer pushforward. The pushforward
279	coercions (`pushforwardComp ≫ eqToHom`) collapse sectionwise by `Scheme.Modules.hom_ext; intro U; rfl`
280	exactly as the `hpf` helper of `pushPullMap_id`. It is a longer (~150-LOC) pentagon calculation and is
281	left for a focused follow-up pass; assembling `pushPullFunctor` needs it together with `pushPullMap_id`.
282	
283	The mate core that splits the composite unit is isolated as the reusable lemma
284	`pushPull_unit_mate` below (axiom-clean): it rewrites the head
285	`p_*(η^f) ≫ pushforwardComp` as `η^{f≫p} ≫ (f≫p)_*(pullbackComp⁻¹)`.
286	
287	**iter-271 breakthrough — the kernel `whnf` wall is now BYPASSED.** The earlier
288	blocker was a kernel `whnf` blow-up when cancelling `pushPullMap`'s two over-triangle
289	`eqToHom` transports *in situ* (at the concrete `pushforward`/`pullback` comparison
290	objects). The reusable brick `pushPull_transport_cancel` below resolves it: it states
291	the cancellation with the over-triangle equality `h : gl ≫ p₁ = p₂` as a **free
292	hypothesis**, proven by a single `subst h` on *abstract* objects (transports become
293	`eqToHom rfl = 𝟙`, kernel-cheap), and is applied to `pushPullMap` via `erw` — which
294	rewrites the tail without forcing the kernel to unfold the comparison objects. (Plain
295	`rw` does not fire: `SheafOfModules` comps are defeq-not-syntactic, so `erw` is
296	mandatory.) Applying `erw [pushPull_transport_cancel …]` to all three `pushPullMap`
297	occurrences (LHS once, RHS twice) was verified to fire with NO kernel blow-up, leaving
298	both sides transport-light. `pushforwardComp` is `Iso.refl` (strictly functorial), so
299	its comparison factor is `𝟙` and is absorbed by the same `erw`.
300	
301	**Remaining work for `pushPullMap_comp` — a clean post-`subst` pentagon (no kernel
302	issues).** After the three `erw`s, `subst`-ing the two free over-triangles (via a
303	generalised auxiliary lemma with `p₂, p₃` and the over-triangles as free hypotheses,
304	mirroring `pushPull_transport_cancel`) clears the remaining transports except the
305	single associativity cell `eqToHom ((kl≫gl₂)≫p₁ = kl≫(gl₂≫p₁))`. The residual goal is
306	the pure pseudofunctor pentagon: it closes by `pushPull_unit_mate kl gl₂` (to convert
307	the composite unit `η^{kl≫gl₂}`) together with
308	`Scheme.Modules.pseudofunctor_associativity (f := kl) (g := gl₂) (h := p₁)` — whose four
309	`pullbackComp` 2-cells (`pullbackComp kl (gl₂≫p₁)`, `pullbackComp gl₂ p₁`,
310	`pullbackComp kl gl₂`, `pullbackComp (kl≫gl₂) p₁`) match exactly the four appearing in
311	the goal. The decompositions `pushforward (a≫b) = pushforward a ⋙ pushforward b` and
312	`(pushforward (a≫b)).map φ = (pushforward b).map ((pushforward a).map φ)` both hold by
313	`rfl`. The only friction left is the whiskered-pentagon + `eqToHom` bookkeeping (the
314	standard defeq-not-syntactic `erw` grind), a ~60-100 LOC follow-up; the kernel obstacle
315	that blocked five prior iterations is gone. -/

317	/-- **Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push–pull head.**
318	For composable scheme morphisms `f : A ⟶ B`, `p : B ⟶ Z` and `N : Z.Modules`, the
319	adjunction unit at `N` for `p` followed by the *head* of `pushPullMap` (the
320	pushforward of the unit for `f`, then the pushforward comparison) equals the unit
321	for the composite `f ≫ p` followed by the pushforward of the inverse pullback
322	comparison. This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
323	`η^{f≫p}` into the iterated units `η^p`, `η^f`; it is the reusable ingredient that
324	the functoriality (pentagon) law of `pushPullMap` repeatedly consumes when
325	splitting a composite unit. Project-local supplement. -/
326	lemma pushPull_unit_mate {A B Z : Scheme.{u}} (f : A ⟶ B) (p : B ⟶ Z)
327	    (N : Z.Modules) :
328	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction p).unit.app N ≫
329	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p).map
330	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f).unit.app
331	            ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p).obj N)) ≫
332	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp f p).hom.app
333	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback f).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p).obj N)) =
334	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (f ≫ p)).unit.app N ≫
335	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (f ≫ p)).map
336	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp f p).inv.app N) := by
337	  have key := unit_conjugateEquiv
338	    ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction p).comp
339	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f))
340	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (f ≫ p))
341	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp f p).inv N
342	  rw [Scheme.Modules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv, Adjunction.comp_unit_app] at key
343	  simpa only [Category.assoc] using key

345	/-- **Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push–pull tail** (kernel-cheap
346	generalised form). The morphism map `pushPullMap` glues its pullback-comparison leg
347	to the target object `pushPullObj F Y₂` by two `eqToHom` coercions along the
348	over-triangle `g.left ≫ Y₁.hom = Y₂.hom`. Cancelling those coercions *in situ*
349	(at the concrete pushforward/pullback objects) provokes a kernel `whnf` blow-up.
350	This lemma states the cancellation **with the over-triangle equality as a free
351	hypothesis** `h : gl ≫ p₁ = p₂`, so the proof is a single `subst h` (after which
352	the transports become `eqToHom rfl = 𝟙` and vanish — kernel-cheap) followed by
353	`simp`. Applying it to `pushPullMap` via `rw` rewrites the tail without forcing the
354	kernel to unfold the comparison objects: the over-triangle leg
355	`eqToHom ≫ (pushforward p₂).map (pullbackComp).hom ≫ eqToHom` collapses to the
356	transport-light `(pushforward (gl ≫ p₁)).map (pullbackComp).hom ≫ eqToHom`, the
357	single residual `eqToHom` carrying the unavoidable object identification of the
358	codomain `pushPullObj F Y₂`. Reusable pre-coherence brick for `pushPullMap_comp`. -/
359	lemma pushPull_transport_cancel {Y₁ Y₂ : Scheme.{u}}
360	    (gl : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁) (p₁ : Y₁ ⟶ X) (p₂ : Y₂ ⟶ X)
361	    (h : gl ≫ p₁ = p₂) (F : X.Modules) :
362	    eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward q).obj
363	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback gl).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p₁).obj F))) h) ≫
364	      (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p₂).map ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp gl p₁).hom.app F) ≫
365	      eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p₂).obj
366	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) h) =
367	    (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (gl ≫ p₁)).map
368	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp gl p₁).hom.app F) ≫
369	      eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward q).obj
370	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) h) := by
371	  subst h
372	  simp

374	/-- **Composite-unit decomposition for the push–pull head.** The adjunction unit
375	`η^{f≫p}` for a composite morphism, expressed through the iterated units `η^p`,
376	`η^f` and the pushforward/pullback comparison isomorphisms. This is the
377	`pushPull_unit_mate` identity solved for `η^{f≫p}` (post-composing with
378	`(f≫p)_*(pullbackComp).hom` cancels the `pullbackComp.inv` factor). Reusable brick
379	for the composition law of `pushPullMap`. -/
380	lemma pushPull_unit_comp {A B Z : Scheme.{u}} (f : A ⟶ B) (p : B ⟶ Z)
381	    (N : Z.Modules) :
382	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (f ≫ p)).unit.app N =
383	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction p).unit.app N ≫
384	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p).map
385	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f).unit.app
386	            ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p).obj N)) ≫
387	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp f p).hom.app
388	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback f).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p).obj N)) ≫
389	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (f ≫ p)).map
390	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp f p).hom.app N) := by
391	  have m := pushPull_unit_mate f p N
392	  erw [reassoc_of% m, ← Functor.map_comp, Iso.inv_hom_id_app,
393	    CategoryTheory.Functor.map_id, Category.comp_id]

395	/-- The pushforward pseudofunctor is *strict* on sheaves of modules: the
396	`pushforwardComp` comparison `2`-cell is the identity on the nose. Holds by `rfl`
397	(`pushforward (a ≫ p) = pushforward a ⋙ pushforward p` definitionally). Project-local
398	collapse used to discharge the pushforward legs of the push–pull pentagon. -/
399	lemma pushforwardComp_hom_app_id {Z₁ Z₂ Z₃ : Scheme.{u}} (a : Z₂ ⟶ Z₁) (p : Z₁ ⟶ Z₃)
400	    (M : Z₂.Modules) : (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp a p).hom.app M = 𝟙 _ :=
401	  rfl

404	hypothesis.** This is the body of `pushPullMap` with the underlying scheme map
405	`a`, the two structure maps `p₁ p₂`, and the over-triangle `w : a ≫ p₁ = p₂`
406	generalised away from the `Over X` packaging. Spelling the over-triangle as a free
407	hypothesis is what makes the composition law provable by `subst` (after which the
408	`eqToHom` over-triangle transports become `eqToHom rfl = 𝟙` and vanish, dodging the
409	kernel `whnf` wall). By construction `pushPullMap F g = rawPushPullMap g.left Y₁.hom

411	noncomputable def rawPushPullMap {Z₁ Z₂ : Scheme.{u}} (a : Z₂ ⟶ Z₁)
412	    (p₁ : Z₁ ⟶ X) (p₂ : Z₂ ⟶ X) (w : a ≫ p₁ = p₂) (F : X.Modules) :
413	    (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p₁).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p₁).obj F) ⟶
414	      (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p₂).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p₂).obj F) :=
415	  (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p₁).map
416	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction a).unit.app
417	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p₁).obj F)) ≫
418	    (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp a p₁).hom.app
419	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback a).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p₁).obj F)) ≫
420	    eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward q).obj
421	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback a).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p₁).obj F))) w) ≫
422	    (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p₂).map ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp a p₁).hom.app F) ≫
423	    eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p₂).obj

432	/- **Composition law `pushPullMap_comp` — reduced to an explicit clean pentagon, not
433	yet closed.** The kernel `whnf` wall that blocked iters 264/265/271 is fully removed:
434	the route is `pushPullMap F g = rawPushPullMap g.left Y₁.hom Y₂.hom (Over.w g) F`
435	(`pushPullMap_eq_raw`, by `rfl`), reducing `pushPullMap_comp` to
436	`rawPushPullMap_comp`, which has the two over-triangles `wg : a ≫ p₁ = p₂`,
437	`wh : b ≫ p₂ = p₃` as **free hypotheses**. `subst wg; subst wh` then makes every
438	`eqToHom` over-triangle transport `eqToHom rfl = 𝟙` and vanish (kernel-cheap — no
439	`whnf` blow-up). After `simp only [rawPushPullMap, eqToHom_refl]`,
440	`rw [pushPull_unit_comp b a _]` (the composite-unit decomposition),
441	`simp only [pushforwardComp_hom_app_id]` (pushforward is *strict*: the comparison
442	2-cells are `𝟙` by `rfl`) and absorbing the resulting defeq-`𝟙`s by
443	`repeat erw [Category.id_comp]`, the goal becomes the genuine **pullback
444	pseudofunctor pentagon** (no transports, no `pushforwardComp`):
445	
446	  `(pushforward p₁).map (a_*(η^b) ≫ (b≫a)_*(pullbackComp b a).hom.app(p₁^*F)) ≫`
447	  `  (pushforward (b≫a≫p₁)).map ((pullbackComp (b≫a) p₁).hom.app F) ≫ eqToHom`
448	  ` = (pushforward (a≫p₁)).map (η^b ≫ (pullback b ⋙ pushforward b).map((pullbackComp a p₁).hom.app F)) ≫`
449	  `  (pushforward (b≫a≫p₁)).map ((pullbackComp b (a≫p₁)).hom.app F)`
450	
451	where the leading `(pushforward p₁).map (η^a)` has already been peeled and the `η^b`
452	units aligned by `(pullbackPushforwardAdjunction b).unit.naturality`. The residual
453	content is `Scheme.Modules.pseudofunctor_associativity (f := b) (g := a) (h := p₁)`
454	(its four `pullbackComp` 2-cells `pullbackComp b a`, `pullbackComp (b≫a) p₁`,
455	`pullbackComp a p₁`, `pullbackComp b (a≫p₁)` match exactly) together with the single
456	associativity cell `eqToHom ((b≫a)≫p₁ = b≫(a≫p₁))`.

458	**Why it is not yet closed (next-prover dead-ends, all hit this iter):** the regime is
459	pervasively *defeq-not-syntactic*. `rw`/`reassoc_of%` of `Category.assoc`,
460	`Functor.map_comp`, the lead `rfl`-bridge, and `pseudofunctor_associativity` all fail
461	to match visually-identical terms (the pushforward/`SheafOfModules` `≫` and `.map`
462	carry non-syntactic instances). `erw` matches but **whnf-unfolds `pullbackComp` into
463	its raw `TwoSquare.equivNatTrans`/`mateEquiv` mate definition**, exploding the goal.
464	`congr 1` on the defeq-but-not-syntactic leading factors recurses into the functor
465	structure and likewise unfolds `pullbackComp` into HEq goals. The promising next route
466	is to reformulate via the adjunction transpose (`(pullbackPushforwardAdjunction _).homEquiv`)

473	/-- **Relative {\v C}ech complex from a cosimplicial nerve** (coherence-free
474	plumbing). Given `f : X ⟶ S` and an augmented cosimplicial object `N` of
475	`O_X`-modules, produce the relative {\v C}ech cochain complex in `QCoh(S)` by:
476	forgetting the augmentation (`CosimplicialObject.Augmented.drop`), pushing the
477	cosimplicial object forward along `f` (`CosimplicialObject.whiskering` applied to
478	`Scheme.Modules.pushforward f`), and taking the alternating coface-map cochain
479	complex (`alternatingCofaceMapComplex`). This is the entire passage `CechNerve ↦
480	CechComplex`, and it uses no `pushforwardComp` / `pullbackComp` coherence — only
481	the (pre)additivity of `S.Modules`. Project-local. -/
482	noncomputable def relativeCechComplexOfNerve (f : X ⟶ S)
483	    (N : CosimplicialObject.Augmented X.Modules) : CochainComplex S.Modules ℕ :=
484	  (AlgebraicTopology.alternatingCofaceMapComplex S.Modules).obj
485	    (((CosimplicialObject.whiskering X.Modules S.Modules).obj
486	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward f)).obj (CosimplicialObject.Augmented.drop.obj N))
487	
488	/-- **Relative Čech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf** (Stacks, Cohomology of
489	Schemes, `lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial`).
490	
491	For `f : X ⟶ S`, a finite affine open cover `𝒰` of `X` (with all intersections
492	affine, e.g. `X` separated) and a quasi-coherent sheaf `F`, the *relative Čech
493	complex* `Č•(𝒰, F)` is the cochain complex of `O_S`-modules with degree-`p` term
494	```
495	  Čᵖ(𝒰, F) = ∏_{(i₀,…,i_p)} (f|_{U_{i₀…i_p}})_* (F|_{U_{i₀…i_p}}),
496	```
497	and differential the alternating sum of the restriction maps
498	`(d s)_{i₀…i_{p+1}} = Σⱼ (-1)ʲ s_{i₀…î_j…i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i₀…i_{p+1}}}`. Over an
499	affine `U = Spec A` with `F|_U = M~` and a standard cover by the `D(fᵢ)`, this is
500	the complex of localisations `∏ M_{f_{i₀}} → ∏ M_{f_{i₀}f_{i₁}} → ⋯`. Each term
501	is quasi-coherent because the intersections are affine and the pushforward of a
502	quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated morphism is
503	quasi-coherent.
504	
505	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
506	`lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial`. -/
507	noncomputable def CechComplex (f : X ⟶ S) (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) (F : X.Modules) :
508	    CochainComplex S.Modules ℕ :=
509	  -- Construction (Stacks): apply the relative pushforward `f_*` over each finite
510	  -- intersection to the Čech nerve `CechNerve 𝒰 F`, then take the alternating-sum
511	  -- Čech differential. This is exactly the coherence-free plumbing
512	  -- `relativeCechComplexOfNerve`, so `CechComplex` is genuinely *defined* in terms
513	  -- of the nerve: an axiom-clean `CechNerve` immediately yields an axiom-clean
514	  -- `CechComplex`. The only remaining hole is `CechNerve` itself.
515	  relativeCechComplexOfNerve f (CechNerve 𝒰 F)
516	
517	/-- **Čech acyclicity on affines** (Stacks 02KG;
518	`lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial` and
519	`lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero`).

261	/-- Given two adjunctions `L₁ ⊣ R₁` and `L₂ ⊣ R₂` both between categories `C`, `D`, there is a
262	bijection between natural transformations `L₂ ⟶ L₁` and natural transformations `R₁ ⟶ R₂`. This is
263	defined as a special case of `mateEquiv`, where the two "vertical" functors are identity, modulo
264	composition with the unitors. Corresponding natural transformations are called `conjugateEquiv`.
265	TODO: Generalise to when the two vertical functors are equivalences rather than being exactly `𝟭`.
266	
267	Furthermore, this bijection preserves (and reflects) isomorphisms, i.e. a transformation is an iso
268	iff its image under the bijection is an iso, see e.g. `CategoryTheory.conjugateIsoEquiv`.
269	This is in contrast to the general case `mateEquiv` which does not in general have this property.
270	-/
271	@[simps!]
272	def conjugateEquiv : (L₂ ⟶ L₁) ≃ (R₁ ⟶ R₂) :=
273	  calc
274	    (L₂ ⟶ L₁) ≃ (𝟭 C ⋙ L₂ ⟶ L₁ ⋙ 𝟭 D) := (Iso.homCongr L₂.leftUnitor L₁.rightUnitor).symm
275	    _ ≃ TwoSquare _ _ _ _ := (TwoSquare.equivNatTrans _ _ _ _).symm
276	    _ ≃ _ := mateEquiv adj₁ adj₂
277	    _ ≃ (R₁ ⋙ 𝟭 C ⟶ 𝟭 D ⋙ R₂) := TwoSquare.equivNatTrans _ _ _ _
278	    _ ≃ (R₁ ⟶ R₂) := R₁.rightUnitor.homCongr R₂.leftUnitor
279

283	      α.app _ ≫ adj₁.counit.app d := by
284	  simp

290	    conv_lhs => rw [← (conjugateEquiv adj₁ adj₂).right_inv α]
291	    exact (conjugateEquiv_counit adj₁ adj₂ ((conjugateEquiv adj₁ adj₂).symm α) d)

302	  simp

307	      adj₂.unit.app c ≫ R₂.map (((conjugateEquiv adj₁ adj₂).symm α).app _) := by
308	    conv_lhs => rw [← (conjugateEquiv adj₁ adj₂).right_inv α]
309	    exact (unit_conjugateEquiv adj₁ adj₂ ((conjugateEquiv adj₁ adj₂).symm α) c)

/-- Clean form of `rawPushPullMap` for a general over-triangle `w : a ≫ p₁ = p₂`:
the transport-free head `(pushforward p₁).map (η^a ≫ a_*(pullbackComp a p₁).hom)`
followed by the single `eqToHom` identifying the target along `w`. Project-local
helper for `pushPullMap_comp`. -/
lemma rawPushPullMap_self_gen {Z₁ Z₂ : Scheme.{u}} (a : Z₂ ⟶ Z₁) (p₁ : Z₁ ⟶ X)
    (p₂ : Z₂ ⟶ X) (w : a ≫ p₁ = p₂) (F : X.Modules) :
    rawPushPullMap a p₁ p₂ w F =
      (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p₁).map
          ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction a).unit.app
              ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p₁).obj F) ≫
            (Scheme.Modules.pushforward a).map
              ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp a p₁).hom.app F)) ≫
        eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward q).obj
          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) w) := by
  subst w
  rw [rawPushPullMap_self]
  simp

input.new_string:
/-- The pure pullback **pentagon** at `F`, in transport-light form: the content of
`pushPullMap_comp` once the units and pushforwards are peeled. It is exactly
`Scheme.Modules.pseudofunctor_associativity (f := b) (g := a) (h := p₁)` evaluated at
`F`, with the associator (an identity on components) absorbed and the two leading
comparison isos inverted. Project-local. -/
lemma pushPull_pentagon {Z₁ Z₂ Z₃ : Scheme.{u}} (a : Z₂ ⟶ Z₁) (b : Z₃ ⟶ Z₂)
    (p₁ : Z₁ ⟶ X) (F : X.Modules) :
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp b a).hom.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p₁).obj F) ≫
        (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (b ≫ a) p₁).hom.app F ≫
        eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)
          (Category.assoc b a p₁)) =
      (Scheme.Modules.pullback b).map ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp a p₁).hom.app F) ≫
        (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp b (a ≫ p₁)).hom.app F := by
  sorry

  have HF := congrArg (fun t => NatTrans.app t F) H
  simp only [NatTrans.comp_app, Functor.whiskerRight_app, Functor.whiskerLeft_app,
    Functor.associator_hom_app, eqToHom_app, Category.assoc, Functor.comp_obj,
    Category.id_comp] at HF
  -- HF : A1⁻¹ ≫ B1⁻¹ ≫ C ≫ D = eqToHom eF, with the associator (= 𝟙) absorbed.
  -- Cancel the two leading isos to isolate `C ≫ D`.
  have key : (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp b a).hom.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p₁).obj F) ≫

          ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp b (a ≫ p₁)).inv.app F ≫
            (Scheme.Modules.pullback b).map ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp a p₁).inv.app F) ≫
              (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp b a).hom.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p₁).obj F) ≫

    simp only [Iso.hom_inv_id_app_assoc, ← Functor.map_comp_assoc, Iso.hom_inv_id_app,
      Functor.map_id, Category.id_comp]
  rw [← Category.assoc, key, HF]
  simp [eqToHom_trans]

Explanation: The rewrite tactic rewrites an expression 'e' using an equality 'a = b' by the following process. First, it looks for all 'a' in 'e'. Second, it tries to abstract these occurrences of 'a' to create a function 'm := fun _a => ...', called the *motive*, with the property that 'm a' is definitionally equal to 'e'. Third, we observe that 'congrArg' implies that 'm a = m b', which can be used with lemmas such as 'Eq.mpr' to change the goal. However, if 'e' depends on specific properties of 'a', then the motive 'm' might not typecheck.

Possible solutions: use rewrite's 'occs' configuration option to limit which occurrences are rewritten, or use 'simp' or 'conv' mode, which have strategies for certain kinds of dependencies (these tactics can handle proofs and 'Decidable' instances whose types depend on the rewritten term, and 'simp' can apply user-defined '@[congr]' theorems as well).

b : Z₃ ⟶ Z₂

Second, it tries to abstract these occurrences of 'a' to create a function 'm := fun _a => ...', called the *motive*, with the property that 'm a' is definitionally equal to 'e'. Third, we observe that 'congrArg' implies that 'm a = m b', which can be used with lemmas such as 'Eq.mpr' to change the goal. However, if 'e' depends on specific properties of 'a', then the motive 'm' might not typecheck.

/-- **Push–pull functor `G` — composition law** (contravariant functoriality).
For composable morphisms `g : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁`, `h : Y₃ ⟶ Y₂` of `X`-schemes,
`G(h ≫ g) = G(g) ≫ G(h)` (writing `≫` for `Over X`-composition; in the informal
`∘`-notation of the blueprint this is `G(g ∘ h) = G(h) ∘ G(g)`). Together with
`pushPullMap_id` this assembles `pushPullObj`/`pushPullMap` into a genuine functor
`(Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules`. Project-local (`lem:push_pull_comp`). -/
lemma pushPullMap_comp (F : X.Modules) {Y₁ Y₂ Y₃ : Over X} (g : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁) (h : Y₃ ⟶ Y₂) :
    pushPullMap F (h ≫ g) = pushPullMap F g ≫ pushPullMap F h := by
  rw [pushPullMap_eq_raw, pushPullMap_eq_raw, pushPullMap_eq_raw]
  exact rawPushPullMap_comp g.left h.left Y₁.hom Y₂.hom Y₃.hom (Over.w g) (Over.w h) F

Second, it tries to abstract these occurrences of 'a' to create a function 'm := fun _a => ...', called the *motive*, with the property that 'm a' is definitionally equal to 'e'. Third, we observe that 'congrArg' implies that 'm a = m b', which can be used with lemmas such as 'Eq.mpr' to change the goal. However, if 'e' depends on specific properties of 'a', then the motive 'm' might not typecheck.

Explanation: The rewrite tactic rewrites an expression 'e' using an equality 'a = b' by the following process. First, it looks for all 'a' in 'e'. Second, it tries to abstract these occurrences of 'a' to create a function 'm := fun _a => ...', called the *motive*, with the property that 'm a' is definitionally equal to 'e'. Third, we observe that 'congrArg' implies that 'm a = m b', which can be used with lemmas such as 'Eq.mpr' to change the goal. However, if 'e' depends on specific properties of 'a', then the motive 'm' might not typecheck.

Possible solutions: use rewrite's 'occs' configuration option to limit which occurrences are rewritten, or use 'simp' or 'conv' mode, which have strategies for certain kinds of dependencies (these tactics can handle proofs and 'Decidable' instances whose types depend on the rewritten term, and 'simp' can apply user-defined '@[congr]' theorems as well).

Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
159

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Know your target in the dependency graph (leandag)
18	
19	The blueprint dependency graph is queryable **read-only** — `archon` is on PATH, `--json` is parseable (the banner goes to stderr). Use it to ground your work instead of guessing:
20	
21	- `archon dag-query node --node <label> --json` — your target's blueprint entry, its `\lean{}` pin, and status. **If the `\lean{}` pin is a `…TODO.…` placeholder, the Lean declaration does not exist yet** — you must *create* it (the typed `sorry`, or the proof if your mode builds it), not "fill" a name that isn't in the environment.
22	- `archon dag-query ancestors --node <label> --json` — your target's full dependency closure. Build on deps that are actually done (`\leanok`); a dependency still marked ∞ / `sorry` is **not** a sound foundation — cite it only if it genuinely typechecks today.
23	- `archon dag-query gaps --json` — the ∞ holes (statements with no informal proof). If your target's cone hits one, the blueprint sketch is incomplete: flag it in your task_result rather than inventing a proof to paper over it.
24	- `archon dag-query unmatched --json` — Lean decls with no blueprint entry. After you add helpers, your new names surface here until they're blueprinted — that is the debt you must report (see Logging → "Needs blueprint entry").
25	
26	The label↔Lean mapping is the `\lean{}` annotation in the blueprint block; `leandag stats` / `leandag focus` also give a project-wide view. You never *write* the graph — that's the dag/plan/review agents' job — but reading it keeps you from proving against a hollow or unfinished foundation.
27	
28	## Protected declarations
29	
30	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
31	
32	## Talking to the user (TO_USER.md)
33	
34	`.archon/TO_USER.md` is a *persistent* shared notice board surfaced to the user as a banner. You may add a bullet **only** for something the user genuinely must act on that you hit while proving — e.g. a missing credential/dependency a sorry is gated on, or a `/- USER: -/` question you cannot resolve. Discipline:
35	
36	- **Concise + relevant**: keep the whole file ≤ 2–3 short bullets; before adding, read it and delete any bullet no longer true. Never a question queue — the loop never waits.
37	- **Concurrency**: provers run in parallel (one per file). To avoid clobbering a sibling lane's bullet, append your single bullet prefixed with your file (`- [Foo/Bar.lean] …`) rather than rewriting the whole file, and only when it's truly user-actionable. Routine "couldn't close this sorry" notes go in `task_results/<your_file>.md`, NOT here.
38	
39	## Avoid early termination
40	
41	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
42	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
43	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
44	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
45	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
46	
47	## Task completion criteria
48	
49	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
50	
51	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
52	2. Zero axioms introduced.
53	3. The file compiles cleanly.
54	
55	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
56	
57	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
58	
59	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
60	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
61	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
62	
63	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
64	
65	## When infrastructure is missing
66	
67	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
68	
69	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
70	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
71	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
72	
73	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
74	
75	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
76	
77	## Proof style
78	
79	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
80	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
81	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
82	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
83	- **List every new declaration you introduce under a `## Needs blueprint entry` heading in your task_result** (name, file, and the facts its proof relies on) — see Logging below. The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: the review agent and planner consume this list (and `archon dag-query unmatched`) to give each new declaration a blueprint block. An unreported helper is an invisible, isolated dependency that silently corrupts the frontier — flagging it is mandatory, not optional. (You never write the blueprint yourself; you only name what needs one.)
84	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
85	
86	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
87	
88	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
89	
90	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
91	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
92	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
93	
94	## LSP MCP tools
95	
96	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
97	
98	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
99	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
100	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
101	
102	## Search protocol
103	
104	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
105	
106	1. `lean_local_search` first.
107	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
108	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
109	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
110	
111	## Tooling traps
112	
113	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
114	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
115	
116	## Logging
117	
118	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
119	
120	```markdown
121	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
122	
123	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
124	### Attempt 1
125	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
126	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
127	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
128	
129	### Attempt 2
130	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
131	- **Result:** RESOLVED
132	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
133	
134	## helper_bijective (line 78)
135	### Attempt 1
136	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
137	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
138	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
139	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
